mappings.rationaleAI: Directly covers the rapid Arab conquests of the 7th-8th centuries, including campaigns after Muhammad's death, conquest of the Sassanid Empire and Byzantine territories, corresponding to outlines B.1.c (Muslim expansion under the Patriarchal Caliphs) and B.2 (Umayyad era westward expansion).

Directly covers the Abbasid Caliphate from 750 CE, the revolution overthrowing the Umayyads, the founding of Baghdad, the House of Wisdom, the translation movement, and the fragmentation of Abbasid power -- matching outline C.1 on the Abbasid caliphate and its cultural life.

The Sassanid Empire provides essential context for pre-Islamic Arabia and the Arab conquest of the Persian Empire, directly relevant to B.1.a (Arabia before Muhammad) and B.1.c.i (conquest of the Sasanid Empire under the early caliphs).

Covers the origins of the Sunni-Shia split after Muhammad's death in 632, the succession crisis between Abu Bakr and Ali, and the Battle of Karbala (680), directly mapping to B.1.c.ii (origins of Shi'ism) and providing context for theological divisions under the Abbasids (C.1.d.i).

The Battle of Tours/Poitiers (732) directly corresponds to outline C.2.a.i, which explicitly mentions the defeat of Muslims near Poitiers by Charles Martel, and reflects B.2.b on the zenith of Umayyad expansion.

Directly covers the history of Muslim Spain (al-Andalus) from the Umayyad conquest (711) through the Cordoban Caliphate, taifa kingdoms, convivencia, and the Reconquista, mapping precisely to outline C.2.a on the Umayyad emirate and caliphate in Spain.

The Almoravid Empire conquered Ghana and unified al-Andalus after the taifa kingdoms, covering the Berber dynasty's rise in northwest Africa and intervention in Spain, relevant to C.2.a.ii (taifa disorders) and C.2.c (other dynasties in North Africa).

The Shahnameh, completed c. 1010, preserves Persian national history and language against Arabic influence, reflecting cultural developments under the Samanid dynasty (C.2.d, Samanid patronage of art and learning) and broader Perso-Islamic civilization.

Al-Kindi (c. 801-873) was central to the Abbasid translation movement, working to bring Greek philosophy into Arabic and reconcile it with Islamic theology, directly relevant to C.1.d on cultural life under the Abbasids, including philosophy and Greek influences.

Directly covers the 8th-10th century Translation Movement at the Abbasid House of Wisdom in Baghdad, the translation of Aristotle, Galen, Ptolemy, and Euclid into Arabic, mapping to C.1.d.ii on Greek and Persian influences in Abbasid arts and sciences.

The Volga Vikings' trade with the Abbasid Caliphate in silver dirhams for furs, slaves, and amber illuminates economic and social life under the Abbasids (C.1.c on manufactures and trade), as documented by Ibn Fadlan.

Byzantium's conflict with the Islamic world, including loss of territories to the Arab conquests and later relations with the Fatimids, provides essential context for B.1.c.i (conquest of Byzantine territories) and C.2.b (Fatimid expansion into Syria).

While the Mamluks ruled from 1250 (later than the section's main period), they emerged from the Turkic slave-soldier system discussed under the late Fatimid and Abbasid successor states, relevant to C.2.f (Turkish dynasties including the Ghaznavids) and connecting to the broader pattern of Turkish military elites in Islamic governance.

Coffee's origins in Ethiopia and Yemen and its spread through the Islamic world provide context for economic and social life in the early Islamic period, relevant to C.1.c on Abbasid economic developments.
